transcript.text: On 275 and 285 and in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Well hi there, I'm Tommy Vance and welcome to the Friday Rock Show.
Now our classic sessions tonight are by the original Fleetwood Mac.
We include tracks from their first ever broadcast in 1967 and some numbers from 69, all with Peter Green.
You can hear classic cuts by The Who, The Damned, Steely Dan, Robert Palmer and Jethro Tull.
And new cuts from albums by XDC, Runner, Orleans and The Bishops.
bishops plus three tracks from the lodger that's the new album by david bowie but tonight we start
with bandit haven't heard the long version like that in ages that's of course the who and the
track called won't get fooled again came out on the 1971 album that was called who's next
and i played it because stephen j thompson of number nine linden terrace and hoik in scotland
sent me a little postcard and said but i played for all the who fans up in that part of the world
including the two Davids and Philip Coutts and Alan.
And Grimble, that's another David, and all his fellow schoolmates, including him,
wishing them luck in their higher exams.
Mr. Thompson then goes on to say,
By the way, do you do voiceovers for adverts?
Well, I have to own up. I do a few thousand every year.
I'm not going to tell you which ones.
And prior to that, you heard something from the new album by Bandit.
It's their second album. The album's called Partners in Crime.
The track was Change of Heart.
And it's nice to know that Mick Gravum, that ace guitarist
who used to be in Procol Harum, has now become a bandit.
There are some people out there who think that is rather sacrilegious.
That was All Along the Watchtower by Frank Marino and Mahogany Rush.
Toulson and I, here in London, in the studio,
think it's rather good, actually, and not sacrilegious at all.
Of course, it was All Along the Watchtower.
And the solo really, note for note, played there
just the way that Hendrix did when he recorded it years and years ago.
Talking about Hendrix, next week, out of the archives,
Jimi Hendrix will reappear on the Friday Rock Show
with a piece of material that hasn't been heard, I think, on radio before.
I think I'm right in saying that.
Frank Marino's album is Tales of the Unexpected.
It's on CBS.
Prior to that, you heard something by a new British band called Runner.
The album is called Runner.
It's out on Acrobat, and the track was Dynamite.
Right, it's 10.23 here on the Friday Rock Show.
On the trail that we've been putting out
with regard to the rebroadcasting of what you're going to hear now,
That is Fleetwood Mac, the original line-up of Fleetwood Mac.
We actually said we had material from 1968 and 1969.
Well, we've actually found, with the assistance of Bernie Andrews,
the great producer, the first ever broadcast
that Fleetwood Mac ever did on BBC Radio 1.
On the 7th of the 11th, 1967, the programme was Top Gear.
So the first two numbers you're going to hear by Fleetwood Mac
are from that period, and the following three numbers are from 1969.
They all include Peter Green.
Here's Fleetwood Mac.
Shall I tell you about my life?
They say I'm a man.
I guess I've got everything that I wouldn't have.
And there's no one I'd rather be.
The good man.
All of my life.
Blames I draw.
I don't want to be sad.
And listening to all that again just reminds me how great a band they really, really were.
That was Fleetwood Mac.
three numbers recorded in 1969
Man of the World
an incredible version of Tallahassee Lassie
and Blues with a Feeling
and prior to that you heard Baby Please Set a Date
and Long Grey Mare that was recorded in 1967
when Radio 1 was just two months old
and put out on the program Top Gear
and all of a sudden I suddenly thought
good heavens, I was doing Top Gear in that day
an age
along with John Peel
on a Sunday afternoon between 2 o'clock and 5
next week on rock on that's not tomorrow that's next week on rock on peter green comes into the
studio and chats about his new album and also what's been happening to him during the great
length of time that he's been away on rock on this saturday at four we feature three of the
world's greatest axemen ted nugent the muhammad ali of rock talks to us about his position in the
world of high energy sound ex genesis guitarist steve hackett comes into our rock on studio to
play tracks from, and review his third solo album.
And from New York, Richie Blackmore reveals what's at the end of his rainbow.
Also, we take a look at the current state of British reggae.
Join me for Radio 1's review of rock,
Rock On, 275, 285, and Stereo VHF,
this Saturday at 4.
How does he talk so fast?
The new single there by XTC that's just come out on Virgin,
it's called Life Begins at the Hop.
And prior to that, one of the all-time great punk records.
For me, the first ever great punk record I ever heard.
By The Damned, it was called Neat, Neat, Neat.
A great track put together by Nick Lowe in the production sense.
And I played that for a gentleman who sent in three cards under three different names, but the same address.
Number four, Parkview, Beeford, Winkley, North Devon in Devon.
I think I'll call you John Vanstone.
John, I hope you enjoy this.
It's 13 minutes now before 11 o'clock.
If there's anything you'd like us to dig out of the archives for you, all you have to do is write to us.
Postcards, letters, money, tins of beans, anything you like.
we don't mind send your communication to the friday rock show bbc radio one london w1a4 ww

I've got to give you lots and lots of love, Smiles, from a lady by the name of Claire.
And also for all the members of the Tiverton School Sixth Form.
This request was sent in by Tom Gamerson, I think it is, of number 25 High Street.
Is that Colompton down there in Devon?
I've been asked to point out by David Lee of Grantham in Lynx
that this piece of music, the Radio 1 theme, is by Van de Graaff Generator.
And it signifies the Friday Night Connection.
Okay, this is the person who's got himself sent right this week.
Andy, you were right, so you get yourself, as I say, a £10 record token.
And as everybody knows, part of the deal is to put together your own Friday Night Connection.
Three pieces of music with a theme.
And I must say, Mr. Haig, these are three ace bits of music.
Can you tell me where my country lies?
Said the unicorn to his true love's eyes.
It lies with me, cried the queen of maybe.
For her merchandise, he traded in his prize.
of hope and glory it's the time of your life easy now take you down chewing through your wimpy dreams
they eat without sound young man says you are what you eat well and says you are what you wear well

We tried three separate albums today.
It must be a pressing fault.
And that was the Friday Night Connection for this week.
Three pieces of music that have a very definite theme running through them.
But it's a crafty one.
A very crafty one that's been put together by Andy Haig.
of No. 54 Lake Road North, Fairhaven, in Lytham in Lancashire.
Crafty fella.
You deserve that £10 you're going to get in record token form.
Right, you think you know what the connection is?
Drop it down on a postcard, send it along to us here.
The Friday Night Connection, Friday Rock Show, BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4WW.
We need to know what the theme was that ran through those pieces of music.
And also, we need your own Friday Night Connection too.
You don't have to, incidentally, tell us what tracks we've played.
Maybe you don't recognize them.
Maybe you just sort of lie there and listen to the lyrics
or maybe figure out the album titles or whatever to figure out the connection.
You don't have to put down on a piece of paper what they are.
But if you do, well, they'll congratulate you even more.
We won't send you any more money because we ain't got it.
It's 21 minutes now past 11.
There's a new album out this Friday.
It's by David Bowie, and here are three cuts from it.
The album's called Lodger,
and this first track is entitled Red Sails.
Coming up on Radio 1 this weekend
will be someone who calls himself Young David Bowie.
David.
I'm going to be on on Sunday at 7 o'clock on Star Special
and I'm going to play a few records that I've picked
and a couple that were suggested to me by Mr...
Today's Grand I.
And we're going to have a bash at putting together a sort of a programme.
Hope you enjoy it. I certainly will.
David Bowie joins the growing ranks of Radio 1 DJs this Sunday at 7pm, star special on national Radio 1.
Now you know. And the tracks that we played from Bowie's latest album, which is called Lodger, were Look Back in Anger and DJ and Red Sails.
And like all the stuff of Mr. Bowie, I think it's never really that immediate. It just seems to grow on you.
And I would like to say that I think the album cover photograph is the least pleasant photograph I think I've ever seen in my life.
Play those tracks for Josh and Mag in Coventry, from Paul, who's in Ipswich, and the messages, you'd better get there tomorrow night.
And also to all the friends of Ashley Holmes, who's at Bruce House in Gordonstoun, that famous school in Elgin, in Mauritia.
He says, summer's only in a few weeks, and well, he'll see you then.
